title: EU Won't Pause EES Despite Summer Airport Delays
excerpt: The EU has refused to suspend its new biometric border system despite five-hour queues at some airports. Here's what travelers to Europe should do this summer.
content:
If you have a trip to Europe booked for the coming weeks, there is a border-control headache you need to plan around: the European Union's new Entry/Exit System (EES) is still causing serious delays at Schengen airports, and Brussels has just confirmed it will not pause the system to ease the summer crunch.

EES is the biometric check that logs fingerprints and a facial scan for non-EU travelers, including US, UK, Canadian, Australian and Indian passport holders, each time they cross a Schengen external border. It became fully operational across the zone on April 10, 2026, after a phased rollout that began the previous October. In theory it replaces manual passport stamping with a faster digital record. In practice, the technology has been inconsistent. Passport-scanning kiosks are frequently malfunctioning, sometimes needing several attempts per traveler that can eat up five minutes each, and when a kiosk fails outright, staff have to fall back to slower manual processing. The result has been wait times ranging from about two minutes at some airports to reported queues of up to five hours at others, with travelers describing gates closing and flights departing only half full because passengers were still stuck in the border line.

Industry groups sounded the alarm well before the summer peak hit. In early July, Airports Council International Europe, Airlines for Europe and the International Air Transport Association jointly wrote to European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen warning that the current implementation was putting airports, airlines and border authorities under "unsustainable pressure" and urging an immediate, temporary suspension. The World Travel and Tourism Council went further, warning that continued disruption could put roughly 41 million arrivals and an estimated $45.4 billion in visitor spending at risk this summer, as some international travelers start reconsidering European trips altogether.

The European Commission's answer, confirmed around July 7-8, was no. Officials called a full suspension "not possible" and "not needed," arguing that having EES enforced in some countries but paused in others would create confusion over travelers' 90-day Schengen stay limits and could see people wrongly flagged as overstayers later. EU Commissioner Magnus Brunner also pushed back on the idea that EES itself is the sole culprit, pointing to "insufficient staff or lack of adequate infrastructure" at the national level as contributing factors. Officials noted that of roughly 1,500 border crossing points across the zone, only about 20 have seen severe congestion, and they expect things to improve gradually as individual countries add staff and hardware.

There is a partial safety valve. Under the existing rollout rules, individual Schengen countries can locally and temporarily pause biometric checks when queues become unmanageable at a specific crossing, and that flexibility window stays open until early September 2026. Portugal has already said it will use six-hour suspensions during peak periods at its airports. But this is a local, ad hoc tool, not the industry-wide pause airlines asked for, and as of this month no country had asked Brussels to extend it further.

So what should you actually do if you're flying into the Schengen area this summer? First, build in a longer buffer than usual for any connecting flight through a Schengen hub, especially at busier airports, since a tight 60-90 minute connection is now a real risk. Second, if you have flexibility in your routing, consider connecting through a non-Schengen country and doing a second, later-in-the-day hop into the Schengen zone, since off-peak arrival slots have generally seen shorter EES lines. Third, keep your passport and any pre-registered biometric details (if your airline or airport offers pre-enrollment) easily accessible, and don't count on being through immigration quickly even with an EES kiosk available. Finally, if you're arriving in July or August specifically, check your arrival airport's own travel advisories close to your departure date, since local suspensions can change on short notice through September. None of this affects your visa-free eligibility or ETIAS timeline, which remains set for a Q4 2026 launch — this is purely about how long you'll stand in line once you land.
